metadata.title: How RLHF Data Is Actually Structured
sources: Bai et al. 2022 — Training a Helpful and Harmless Assistant with RLHF (arXiv:2204.05862)

beats.narration_text: I want to get my hands on how RLHF preference data is actually laid out, because 'human feedback' stays abstract until I see the schema. Help me: (1) describe the fields of a single preference example — the shared prompt, the chosen response, and the rejected response; (2) explain how a reward model turns thousands of these chosen-versus-rejected pairs into a scalar it can score new outputs with; (3) construct one pair where the 'chosen' answer is subtly worse, and reason about how that mislabeled example would corrupt the reward model.

props.command: I want to get my hands on how RLHF preference data is actually laid out, because 'human feedback' stays abstract until I see the schema. Help me: (1) describe the fields of a single preference example — the shared prompt, the chosen response, and the rejected response; (2) explain how a reward model turns thousands of these chosen-versus-rejected pairs into a scalar it can score new outputs with; (3) construct one pair where the 'chosen' answer is subtly worse, and reason about how that mislabeled example would corrupt the reward model.
output: 160k+ preference pairs across helpfulness and harmlessness
Pairwise comparison — no absolute quality scale needed

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The naive approach is to rate responses on a scale of one to ten. It fails immediately. One annotator gives a response a six. Another gives the exact same response an eight. The scale is arbitrary and people don't agree on it. There's no shared definition of what a seven means. Pairwise comparison sidesteps this entirely: instead of rating responses on an absolute scale, you show two responses to the same prompt and ask which is better. Annotators agree on that much more reliably.

props.artifactHeading: People disagree on scales — they agree on comparisons
artifactLines: Annotator A rates a response: 6 out of 10
Annotator B rates the same response: 8 out of 10
No shared definition of what '7' means — scores are arbitrary
Pairwise: 'Which of these two responses is better?' — much more reliable
Comparison removes the need for an absolute quality scale

beats.narration_text: The HH-RLHF dataset structure is elegant in its simplicity. Each line in the jsonl file is a dictionary with exactly two keys: chosen and rejected. Both values are full conversation threads — a sequence of human and assistant turns leading up to two different responses to the same final prompt. The chosen response is the one the human annotator preferred. The rejected response is the one they didn't. Same prompt, same context, two continuations, one winner. That's the whole thing.

props.artifactHeading: Each line: one chosen response, one rejected response
artifactLines: JSONL format — one preference pair per line
Two keys: 'chosen' and 'rejected'
Each value: full conversation thread (human + assistant turns)
Same prompt context, two different continuations
Human annotator picked one — that's the 'chosen'

beats.narration_text: The dataset has two tracks, collected separately. Helpfulness pairs test whether responses are useful, accurate, and appropriately detailed. Harmlessness pairs test whether responses refuse or enable harmful requests. Within helpfulness, the data comes in three tranches: base model responses, responses filtered by rejection sampling against an early preference model, and responses from the online training process. Each tranche gets progressively better — you can see the preference model improving by how often annotators pick the later-generation responses.

artifactLines: HELPFULNESS: useful, accurate, appropriately detailed responses
HARMLESSNESS: refuses or enables harmful requests
Helpfulness tranche 1: base model (52B context-distilled) responses
Tranche 2: rejection-sampled (best-of-16) against early preference model
Tranche 3: online RLHF — model improves with each iteration

beats.narration_text: The preference model is trained directly on these pairs. For each chosen response, it learns to output a higher reward scalar. For each rejected response, a lower one. The optimization objective is a contrastive loss: push the chosen score up, push the rejected score down, widen the gap. Once the preference model is trained, you use it to score new model outputs during RL fine-tuning — reinforcing responses that score high and discouraging those that score low. The crucial limit: the preference model learns which response is better, not why.

props.artifactHeading: Contrastive loss — push chosen up, push rejected down
artifactLines: Chosen response → reward model outputs high scalar (e.g. 0.8)
Rejected response → reward model outputs low scalar (e.g. 0.2)
Loss: maximize gap between chosen and rejected scores
Preference model then scores new outputs during RL fine-tuning
Limitation: learns which is better — not why it is better

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Ask Claude: 'I have two responses to the same prompt. Response A is accurate but terse. Response B is warm and detailed but contains one factual error. Write a JSONL preference pair entry where Response A is chosen. Then explain what a preference model trained on this pair will and won't learn.' That prompt makes the chosen-rejected structure concrete and forces Claude to reason about what signal the pair actually encodes — which is exactly the gap between the data format and the training objective. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: Write a JSONL preference pair where a terse accurate response beats a warm but factually wrong one. Then explain what a preference model trained on this pair will and won't learn.
props.segment: How RLHF Data Is Actually Structured
output: Claude writes a valid chosen/rejected JSONL entry
Explains what the reward model learns: accuracy over warmth
Names the blind spot: it learns preference, not explanation

beats.narration_text: How RLHF Data Is Actually Structured
